actions.title: Effective Instruction
actions.description:
(Student Ownership Framework: Instruction) All learning is driven by
highly engaging, effective, and efficient instruction.
Support student learning by recruiting and retaining highly qualified
teachers, administrators, and classified support staff.
● Fully credentialed and appropriately assigned teachers will
implement effective instruction to support all students toward
meeting academic goals. Teachers will implement a Learning
Model that incorporates the elements of best, first instruction.
● Site administrators will manage school operations and serve as
instructional leaders.
● Classified support staff will provide efficient and reliable
support to school operations and contribute to a welcoming
environment for students and parents.
● Other district personnel will support schools in providing quality
programs and ensure all county, state, and federal regulations
are met.
● Maintain a competitive salary schedule to recruit highly
qualified staff who will work to achieve positive student
outcomes.
● Continue to monitor teacher assignments to ensure appropriate
placement based on credentials.

actions.title: Instructional Support Services
actions.description:
Provides instructional support personnel to support student needs in
the classroom.
* Provides instructional support personnel to support the needs
of English learners, particularly newcomers at the Emerging
level of language proficiency, where feasible.
* Ensure that English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth have access to the full range of college preparatory
courses.
* Increase access to A-G courses and provide support for
student success in A-G courses.
* Provide teacher professional development support through
teacher facilitators.
LEA-wide instructional support staff services are principally directed to
support identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and
foster youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. Services
will benefit educationally disadvantaged youth in need of additional
intervention support, smaller instructional grouping strategies, and
differentiated instruction. This contributing action will address the gaps
in performance on the academic indicator and graduation rate for EL,
FY, LI students.

actions.title:
Implementation of State
Standards
actions.description:
(Student Ownership Framework: Curriculum) All student learning is
driven by a standards-based curriculum with measurable and
achievable outcomes.
All students will have appropriate standards-aligned textbooks and
instructional materials, including digital instructional materials, and will
be enrolled in all required areas of study. Core competencies will be
aligned through the development of a standards-based scope and
sequence for each course.

actions.title:
Resources to Support Course
Access
actions.description:
Maintain instructional materials, supplies, and/or other resources to
support classroom needs in all content areas. LEA-wide resources to
support course access are principally directed to support identified
needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster youth, and
contribute to increased/improved services. This action supports
students by increasing and improving the opportunities for hands-on
learning and real-world application of concepts taught in content
areas. Expenditures in this action are intended to:
● Improve program quality, student engagement, and student
access to programs.
● Support program needs in all content areas to provide a well-
rounded education, including, but not limited to: English language
arts, academic language/writing skills, English language
development, mathematics, science, history/social studies, world
language programs, physical education, visual and performing
arts, and Career Technical Education (CTE) programs.
● Supplement support for students with specific needs (including
specialized program curriculum and support), including academic,
behavioral, social-emotional, and other wellness needs.
● For school level plans, please visit school websites, listed on page
one of this plan.
This contributing action will improve equity and access to educational
resources for EL, FY, LI students.

actions.title:
New Teacher Induction and
Support
actions.description:
The HBUHSD Induction Program provides our candidates with an
individualized, job-embedded system of mentoring, support, and
professional learning for every teacher in their first two years in the
profession. Beginning in their first year of teaching, our program
guides candidates towards clearing their preliminary teaching
credential as we prepare and inspire our candidates to meet the
needs of all students through innovative learning. Candidates focus
on strategies for working with different student populations and on
instructional practices for differentiating instruction. The program is
designed to support candidates in meeting each standard of the
California Standards for the Teaching Profession.

actions.title:
Professional Development and
Support for Certificated and
Classified Staff
actions.description:
HBUHSD will continue to provide foundational professional
development for certificated and classified staff. HBUHSD will
develop and provide a cohesive program of district- and site-based
professional learning to support student success.
Areas of focus for professional development include:
● State academic standards and content specific professional
learning for effective instructional practices.
● Teacher capacity and instructional leadership for specific
district/school priority initiatives and programs.
● Professional development services for administrators.
● Collaboration with instructional staff to evaluate and modify
instructional practices.
● Opportunities for classified staff based on interests and
district/school needs.

actions.title:
Information and Technology
Services
actions.description:
The mission of the Information and Technology Services (ITS)
Department is to provide innovative, contemporary, and accessible
technology in computing, media, and telephone services to enable the
student, parents, faculty, and staff to effectively meet their goals as a
learning community. To accomplish this mission, ITS works
collaboratively with the HBUHSD community to provide technological
leadership that emphasizes empowerment of the individual through
the use of technology.
ITS is responsible for all of the technology in the District. ITS caters to
the diverse technology needs of students, parents, and employees.
ITS provides technical support, training, resources, and services to
help everyone at HBUHSD to work smarter, faster, and safer. As
technology impacts virtually everyone in the District and at home, ITS
provides vision and delivery of technology services essential to the
operation and success of the District.

actions.title:
Instructional Technology Tools
and Library/Media Resources to
Enhance Teaching and
Learning
actions.description:
Continue to ensure equity and access to technology tools and
learning supports for all students. Instructional technology and
library/media services are principally directed to support the identified
needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster youth, and
contribute to increased/improved services.
Instructional Technology:
● Continue to provide all students with a digital device, such as a
tablet/laptop.
● Strategically utilize digital tools and platforms across all subject
areas to enable personalized instruction, provide data-informed
interventions, promote student creativity and collaboration, and
cultivate the technological fluencies necessary for navigating
our digital age.
● Develop policies, professional development, and support
focused on the effective, safe, and fair use of AI-enabled
technology.
● Conduct a comprehensive audit and analysis of all current
technology programs to assess usage, effectiveness, potential
redundancies, and safety/privacy considerations.
● Develop and implement systematic policies for sunsetting
outdated, underutilized or insecure tools, while establishing
clear criteria and procedures for adopting, implementing, and
renewing high-impact digital resources aligned with curricular
needs and robust safety/privacy protocols.
● Implement an integrated instructional program focusing on
digital citizenship to empower students with the knowledge and
mindsets to navigate the online world safely, think critically
about technology's impacts, and leverage digital tools in a
responsible and productive manner throughout their academic
and professional careers.
Library/Media Services
● Continue to provide extended time to utilize district and site
resources for students such as extended library hours and
access to printing.
● Include digital literacy/responsibility, social media competency,
responsible digital citizenship and search protocols, and
considerations around artificial intelligence (AI).
● Provide materials from a variety of reading levels and in a
variety of formats. Create special collections to support school
programs and student interests.
● Continue to provide digital library access through effective
asset management systems, digital books and audiobooks,
digital databases, and more. Increase usage of state-released
databases.
This contributing action will improve equity and access to instructional
technology and library/media resources for EL, FY, LI students.

actions.title: Data and Assessment
actions.description:
(Student Ownership Framework: Assessment) All student learning is
driven by regular assessment that guides instructional decision-
making.
Analyze formative and summative assessment results to inform
instruction and to identify students with targeted instructional needs.
Support the use of formative assessments and planned data checks
of specific learning and implement a data-analysis protocol to
measure student learning and inform instruction.
Provide evaluation services for the collection of data and the
completion of evaluation reports for local schools, the district, and
state. Use multiple state and local metrics to measure achievement
and progress.
Data and assessment services are principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. Data is used to
identify needs, inform instructional practices, and to evaluate
effectiveness of programs, particularly to address the disparities in
data as identified with at-promise students, including English learners,
low-income students, and foster youth. This contributing action will
address the gaps in performance on the academic indicator and
graduation rate for EL, FY, LI students.

actions.title:
Multi-Tiered System of Support
(MTSS)
actions.description:
MTSS lead teachers at each comprehensive school site serve as
instructional coaches and will coordinate processes to ensure the
alignment of systems necessary for all students’ academic,
behavioral, and social success at their school site. MTSS-related
support, strategies, and programs are principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in performance on the
academic indicator and graduation rate for EL, FY, LI students.
● Promote opportunities for teachers and administrators to
receive professional development supporting the
implementation of the MTSS Framework.
● Provide professional development to MTSS lead teachers on
instructional coaching to support the implementation of Tier I
instructional strategies.
● Ensure progress monitoring for all students, with a focus on
early intervention.
● Continue to implement evidence-based programs, strategies,
and student interventions.
● Expand credit attainment options and continue to utilize
research-based math and reading programs to close
achievement gaps.
● Implement programs and practices to support the social-
emotional, wellness, and mental health needs of students.
● Coordinate available programs offered by the district, school,
and community-based organizations to support the social-
emotional needs of students.
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Huntington Beach HS/English Learners/Math (M1.5, M1.9, M1.10,
M2.14)
● Implement effective MTSS monitoring and tracking to quickly
identify students in need of additional supports, with an
emphasis on English learners.
● Use MTSS Mentors to provide instructional support for
students in classes and implement student conferencing to
review progress, assign targeted interventions, and/or help
students stay on track or get back on track.

goals.description:
HBUHSD will provide quality educational programs and services to increase academic
achievement and college/career readiness for all students.

actions.title: College Readiness
actions.description:
HBUHSD schools will provide programs and services that support
students’ college readiness and exploration of post-secondary
options. Programs and services include dual enrollment and
other college credit course opportunities, the California College
Guidance Initiative, and guidance services.
● Expand the use of college readiness tools to inform and
align students' strengths and interests to post-secondary
goals.
● Support costs associated with textbooks, materials, and
fees for dual enrollment and college credit course
opportunities for students during the summer, fall, and
spring semesters.
● Support professional development and collaboration
opportunities related to the implementation of the district’s AP/IB
program.
● Support student access to counselors for early and more
frequent college advisement.
LEA-wide college readiness initiatives are principally directed to
support identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and
foster youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in A-G completion and
support equity and college access for EL, FY, LI students.
(Note: Planned expenditures are coordinated with the Career Access
Pathways Grant (CCAP) and A-G Completion Improvement Block
Grant funds)

actions.title: Career Readiness
actions.description:
Expand career readiness through awareness and student
participation in sequenced pathways aligned to careers. Career
Technical Education (CTE) pathway courses help prepare
students for post-secondary career paths in a range of high-wage
and in-demand careers, engaging students in high-interest
courses, providing choices to explore potential future job options, and
preparing students with a strong foundation toward entry in a technical
school or college/university upon graduation.
Provide students with access to programs of study that align with state
board-approved career technical education standards. CTE classes
and the relevant experiences embedded in these classes help educate
and expose students to the reality that engaging in the workforce and
preparing for college need not be mutually exclusive as evidenced by
students' participation in work-based learning opportunities through the
district's 47 CTE pathways.
● Expand CTE Community College Pathway Days.
● Integrate CTSOs (Career Technical Student Organization) into
CTE pathways. The mission of a CTSO is to enhance student
learning through contextual instruction, leadership and personal
development, applied learning and real-world application.
● Create marketing videos and materials to promote programs
and educate students, families, and the communities about the
various programs available.
● Support the organization of high-quality experiences that help
students to identify and explore their career interests and
prepare for career options through practical experience (work-
based learning continuum).
● Expand the number of industry partners and internship
opportunities available to students, with a focus on offering a
broader range of career sectors and ensuring equitable access
across all schools
LEA-wide career readiness initiatives are principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in A-G completion for EL, FY,
LI students, address equity and access, and provide additional
opportunities for career exploration and preparation.
[Note: Planned expenditures are coordinated with CTEIG, Perkins, K12
SWP, K-16 Collaborative, GSPP, and AIG.]

actions.title:
Advancement Via Individual
Determination (AVID)
actions.description:
Continue to offer the Advancement Via Individual Determination (AVID)
program. Support AVID implementation to close opportunity gaps by
focusing on key foundational elements of AVID:
● Building teacher capacity for embedding AVID strategies in
instructional practice.
● Explicit teaching of skills and techniques for academic success.
● Creating an environment of high expectations.
● Providing one-to-one and/or small group tutorials.
● Maintaining strong student-teacher relationships.
● Building a culture of college and career readiness.
● Enhancing the AVID experience by supporting college
visits/tours and other activities that promote the college-going
culture.
● Supporting professional development and collaboration
opportunities related to the implementation of the district’s AVID
program.
LEA-wide AVID implementation is principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in A-G completion and post-
secondary enrollment rates for EL, FY, LI students.

actions.title: AP/IB Support
actions.description:
Supplement funding for low-income students who take Advanced
Placement (AP) and International Baccalaureate (IB) exams. This is a
‘limited’ contributing action supporting low-income students.

actions.title: STEAM
actions.description:
Implement STEAM (Science, Technology, Engineering, Arts,
Mathematics) education initiatives. Increase opportunities to promote
student interest in STEAM careers and engagement in STEAM related
courses, programs, and activities. Support student participation in
STEAM related courses, clubs/organizations, and competitions. To
maintain a relevant and high-quality program, assess needs to acquire
STEAM-related materials, supplies, and equipment to support Next
Generation Science Standards.
LEA-wide STEAM initiatives are principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in pupil engagement metrics
for EL, FY, LI students, including chronic absenteeism, suspension
rates, and dropout rates.
(Note: Expenditures in this action are embedded in Goal 1, Action 4,
and supported based on site-level needs and student interests.)

actions.title: English Learner Supports
actions.description:
The English Learner (EL) program will continue to ensure that English
learners can meet the annual English language development
objectives. English learners will have full access to the State Academic
Standards and the English Language Development (ELD) standards to
gain academic knowledge and English language proficiency. All
English learners receive both designated and integrated ELD support
and access to the standard instructional program with instruction
designed for English learners to develop proficiency in English as
rapidly and effectively as possible. Where feasible, a school may
establish Emerging Pathway course options for newcomers who have
been in US schools for less than two years. The HBUHSD Master
Plan for English Learners provides the structure for program
implementation and evaluation for all students with varied language
backgrounds who are served in these programs. (Required program
costs are embedded in Goal 1, Action 1.)
Contributing actions include:
● Support for English learners to achieve annual progress
includes use of bilingual instructional support personnel,
implementation of supplemental curriculum, and additional
materials and supplies.
● The district ELD Facilitator and site-level PELL Coordinators will
coordinate and provide EL support services, including
professional development, coaching and support for teachers,
progress monitoring of ELs and assignment of interventions as
needed, parent engagement opportunities, and/or other
supports as determined by site needs.
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
HBUHSD (District)/English Learners/ELA
(M2.9, M2.10, M2.11, M2.13)
● Continue to implement the district’s multi-year professional
development plan specific to the implementation of programs
and services for English learners (E.L. Achieve).
● Build internal capacity for ongoing coaching and support for
teachers in supporting Constructing Meaning.
● Incorporate a specific instructional focus for Reflective Learning
Walks that embeds integrated ELD strategies, including support
for teacher leaders and administrative teams leading the work.
● Facilitate English Learner progress monitoring systems
implemented districtwide.
● Support the implementation of EL instructional coaches focused
on providing coaching and professional development, and direct
student support for ELs.

actions.title:
Long Term English Learner
Supports
actions.description:
English Learners shall be designated as Reclassified Fluent English
Proficient (RFEP) when they have acquired the English language skills
necessary to receive instruction and achieve academic progress, at a
level equivalent to students of the same age or grade whose primary
language is English. Until reclassified, all English learners receive
ELD instruction, unless the parent exercises their right to decline
participation. Long Term English Learners (LTEL) are those English
learners who have been enrolled in a U.S. school for six or more years
and have not yet met criteria for reclassification. The district convened
a team of administrators and teacher leaders to discuss the unique
needs of LTEL students and implications for programs. The following
areas will support LTEL student progress:
● Assist LTEL students with their academic and non-academic
needs and connect them to other school resources, including
tutoring and instructional support, instructional resources, and
wellness support and resources.
● Support teachers and administration in meeting the needs of
their LTEL students and contributing to a more inclusive
community, including the implementation of assessment and
progress monitoring, full access to A-G and CTE, district and
site PEL Coordinator support, and parent and family
engagement opportunities.
● Provide quality programs specialized in strategies for supporting
English language acquisition. Whenever feasible, create LTEL
specific sections for designated ELD. Programs for designated
ELD may be embedded within intentionally designed elective-
driven content. Designated ELD will also be piloted as a
component of Study Skills X, to support dually identified
students.
● Provide teachers opportunities for professional development
focused on the advancement of specialized instruction.
● Empower LTEL students to become full participants in their
school community through opportunities to build school
connectedness, student engagement, connection, belonging,
cultural awareness, significance, friendship, and community.

actions.title:
Homeless and Foster Youth
Supports
actions.description:
Provide supplementary services for foster and homeless youth.
Maintain a district-level homeless youth liaison and a liaison at each
school site. Maintain ongoing collaboration with key staff in county
child welfare agency, mental health agencies, county office of
education foster youth services program, foster family agencies and
other placement providers, and other entities providing care and
services to foster youth within the district.
● Continue ongoing review of best practices for supporting the
needs of foster youth.
● Support school administrators in developing a cohesive and
comprehensive plan on how to onboard and continually support
foster youth students.
● Continue to identify the homeless and foster 11th and 12th
graders and meet with each student to educate them regarding
the graduation credits exemption.
● Allocate funds for academic supports and remediation;
transportation; and costs and fees for sports and extracurricular
programs.
● Provide professional development for site teachers to work
effectively with homeless and foster youth.
● Annually update a resource guide for homeless and foster youth
for use by staff and families.
● Partner with agencies to provide services to targeted sites to
provide resources, counseling, etc. for students experiencing
homelessness.
● Contract with agencies providing specific case management
services, as appropriate.
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Fountain Valley HS/Homeless/Suspension (M3.5)
Marina HS/Homeless/Suspension (M3.5)
● Refine standardized processes for handling cases that may
result in suspension. District and site liaisons will be included in
suspension meetings. Review protocols with administrative
teams.
● Consider alternatives to suspension, depending on type of
infraction, including the implementation of restorative practices,
conflict mediation, and diversion programs. Provide
opportunities for staff to be trained in programs.
Valley Vista HS/Homeless/College and Career, Graduation
(M1.1, M2.2, M2.7)
● Improve systems for student progress monitoring and support
by implementing Goal 4, Action 1: Student Support Facilitator.
● Full-time social workers from Project Hope Alliance and Stand
Up For Kids work one-on-one with students to develop plans
designed to address barriers to academic progress and high
school graduation, including academic support, mentoring,
coordination with teachers for progress monitoring, connections
to external resources (such as housing and healthcare), parent
involvement, college and career support, and 24/7 access to
case managers.
This is a ‘limited’ contributing action supporting foster youth and low-
income/homeless students.
(Note: Planned expenditures for items of cost are embedded in Goal 1,
Action 4)

actions.title: Special Education Supports
actions.description:
The HBUHSD has gained recognition for providing a full spectrum of
special programs and alternative settings for students with
Individualized Education Plans (IEPs). With programs like the Special
Abilities Cluster (SAC); Vocational Opportunities with Instruction and
Community Experiences for Students (VOICES); Pathways;
Independence, Diversity, Educating All Students (IDEAS); and
Endeavors; students with special needs have the opportunity to
receive instruction in accordance with their individual needs and to
participate in activities a comprehensive school site has to offer.
● Maintain and provide co-teaching classes and the number of
co-teaching sections based on student needs in order to
support the least restrictive environment. Co-teaching
strategies to meet student needs supporting the least restrictive
environment.
● Provide professional development for special education case
managers regarding graduation requirements and college
requirements.
● Continue to provide professional development around data-
informed IEP development, with connections between abilities
and goals, and documents to support for students upon exiting
high school.
● Increase opportunities for students with disabilities to enroll in
A-G eligible courses.
● Assess needs for tools (such as assistive technology and
supportive software) to support students with disabilities.
● Continue to implement Unified Programs (e.g., Unified Sports,
United Sound, Unified Cheer, and Unified Arts) to promote
social inclusion through shared training and shared
experiences.
● Parents of students with disabilities will continue to have the
opportunity to participate in their child's education through
events like the Community Advisory Committee and the District
College Night.
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
HBUHSD (District)/Students with Disabilities/Math (M2.16)
Fountain Valley HS/Students with Disabilities/ELA & Math (M2.15,
M2.16)
Huntington Beach HS/Students with Disabilities/ELA & Math (M2.15,
M2.16)
Ocean View HS/Students with Disabilities/ELA & Math (M2.15, M2.16)
Westminster HS/Students with Disabilities/College and Career (M2.7)
Marina HS/Students with Disabilities/College and Career
Ocean View HS/Students with Disabilities/College and Career
● Increase opportunities for special education teachers to
collaborate with subject area specific general education
teachers and site administrators.
● Include special education teachers in the Constructing Meaning
(CM) cohort or provide other opportunities for special education
teachers to collaborate around the application of CM strategies
for students with disabilities.
● Implement effective MTSS monitoring and tracking to quickly
identify students in need of additional supports, with an
emphasis on students with disabilities.
● Provide small group settings for targeted support, including
before and after school support and lunchtime support
opportunities (HBHS).
● Plan a Summer Bridge intervention class for incoming 9th
graders to improve the transition to high school math
coursework (HBHS, OVHS, WHS)
● Build a common understanding around the importance and
benefits of state testing with all educational partners, to support
high test participation rates, avoiding test participation rate
penalties.
● Support the implementation of interim assessments for ELA,
math, and science. Prepare students to gain familiarity with the
testing interface and tools.
● Practice the application of designated supports and
accommodations.
● Schedule time for the analysis and interpretation of data related
to student test results. Schedule time for teams to review
curriculum, pacing, and standards for alignment with state
assessments.
● Build upon the use of IXL math as a diagnostic tool. IXL
provides powerful insights that help teachers make more
effective instructional decisions every day. With data from IXL's
curriculum and the Real-Time Diagnostic, IXL Analytics makes it
easy to amplify the impact on student growth.
● Review impact of math pathways for students with disabilities to
ensure proper placement and course offerings. (i.e., WHS
Geometry X, Algebra 1X, Consumer Math X).
(Note: Planned expenditures are coordinated with state and federal
funds for special education.)

actions.title: Wellness
actions.description:
Provide appropriate student supports for social-emotional, mental,
behavioral, nutritional, and physical wellness. Student supports are
provided by staff, including wellness specialists, school psychologists,
student support school psychologists, college and career specialists,
school counselors, nurses, speech and language pathologists, vision
and audiological services, and food services staff. (Personnel costs
are non-contributing for school psychologists, nurses, speech and
language pathologists, vision and audiological services, and food
services staff.)
● Implement wellness centers and/or promote other wellness
initiatives and activities to support students’ socio-emotional,
mental, behavioral, nutritional, and physical wellness.
● Embrace the community schools model for increasing equitable
student learning outcomes and supporting student needs by
strengthening family and community foundations with
approaches that sustain mental and behavioral health through
alignment of resources. Schools will review site-based needs
and promote resources to support student wellness.
LEA-wide wellness initiatives are principally directed to support
identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and foster
youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in pupil engagement metrics
for EL, FY, LI students, including chronic absenteeism, suspension
rates, and dropout rates.
This action also supports staff wellness initiatives (without
expenditures of the supplemental budget reserved for student
wellness). Schools will promote and implement initiatives with the
understanding of how staff wellness contributes to overall school
climate and positive student outcomes.

actions.title:
Safe and Supportive Learning
Environments
actions.description:
Ensure that school campuses are safe and supportive environments
by implementing programs and practices that yield positive student
outcomes.
* Continue to develop and implement programs and strategies
that support positive school climate, including inclusive
practices, restorative practices, trauma-informed practices, and
related training for staff and students. Continue to promote
“Name, Face, Story” as an important component of building
inclusive communities and a positive climate on campuses.
* Develop systems and processes that facilitate and promote
attendance recovery and attendance improvement.
* Provide resources related to digital literacy, social media and
cyber citizenship and respond effectively to safety and security
issues as they relate to technology usage.
* Continue to inform parents and students about programs that
allow for open flow communication with respect to safety
concerns on campus, including anonymous reporting.
LEA-wide safe and supportive school initiatives are principally directed
to support identified needs of English learners, low-income students,
and foster youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in pupil engagement metrics
for EL, FY, LI students, including chronic absenteeism, suspension
rates, and dropout rates.

actions.title:
School Connectedness and
Student Voice
actions.description:
Provide opportunities and increased access for students to be
involved and engaged in school (clubs/organizations, athletics, special
programs, school activities, assemblies, educational trips, etc.).
* Provide schools with guidance, support, and resources to
increase students’ sense of belonging and school
connectedness.
* Promote student voice as a strategy for contributing to student
engagement, trust, and equity.
LEA-wide school connectedness initiatives are principally directed to
support identified needs of English learners, low-income students, and
foster youth, and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will address the gaps in pupil engagement metrics
for EL, FY, LI students, including chronic absenteeism, suspension
rates, and dropout rates.

actions.title: Parent & Family Engagement
actions.description:
Plan high-quality parent and family engagement opportunities.
Consider barriers to participation, including communication/
outreach, interpretation needs, childcare needs, transportation,
meeting times, type of meeting (i.e., in-person, virtual, or hybrid),
recruitment, retention, and acknowledgement for participation.
● Provide information on post-secondary options and
educational resources unique to each grade level.
● Expand the use of college readiness tools to inform
parents and align students' strengths and interests to post-
secondary goals.
● Survey families to determine topics of interest and offer
Parent University workshops on relevant topics.
● Provide educational opportunities through Parent University,
site communications, parent groups, and classes.
● Continue to provide training on how to navigate the
parent/student portal for families.
● Increase availability, awareness, and access to family education
programs to support EL achievement.
● Provide drug and alcohol awareness and student
wellness education.
● Increase outreach efforts to encourage parental participation in
site and district decision making.
LEA-wide parent and family engagement initiatives are principally
directed to support identified needs of English learners and low-income
students and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will improve parent and family engagement
perceptions included in our annual LCAP survey for EL and LI
students.

actions.title: Communication & Outreach
actions.description:
Promote positive school and district programs, services, and
resources to increase student, family, and community awareness. In
addition to other methods of communication, use technology and
media to increase the potential for parent/student participation related
to curricular and co-curricular programs. Evaluate barriers to
participation and provide communication and outreach services to
inform parents/families about school and district programs,
events/activities, resources, and other involvement opportunities.
Provide translation and interpretation services as requested and/or as
necessary.
LEA-wide communication and outreach initiatives are principally
directed to support identified needs of English learners and low-income
students and contribute to increased/improved services. This
contributing action will improve parent and family engagement
perceptions included in our annual LCAP survey for EL and LI
students.

actions.title:
Well-Maintained School
Facilities
actions.description:
Provide resources for operational/facility needs to ensure a safe,
secure, clean, and functional learning and working environment. This
includes staffing for plant/maintenance/custodial services,
maintenance/custodial supplies and equipment, and utilities costs.
* Continue to maintain facilities to ensure a clean and safe
environment for students.
* Foster learning environments aligned with 21st century skills and
teaching methods.
* Provide safe, efficient transportation services to eligible
students for home-to school transportation services and other
co-curricular activities.
* Continue the annual review of school safety plans and facilities
inspections.
* Improve tracking and reliable service of Maintenance and
Operations work orders through the implementation of a central
hub using the Facilitron system.

actions.title: Academic Climate
actions.description:
(Student Ownership Framework: Climate) All student learning is driven
by a positive academic climate.
* Maintain respectful, cooperative, and collaborative academic
environments that provide a high level of engagement with
student ownership as the driver.

goals.description:
Valley Vista High School (VvHS) will increase graduation rates by 3% annually or maintain
a rate of 68% or higher by the 2025-26 school year. VvHS will make progress in other
student outcomes by providing a positive environment conducive to learning and creating
authentic and meaningful learning experiences that will ensure high levels of student
motivation, student engagement, and school connectedness.

actions.title: Student Support Systems
actions.description:
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance based on the
2023 Dashboard base year:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, Socio-Economically Disadvantaged,
Hispanic/ELA and Math (M4.7/M4.8)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White/Graduation (M4.2)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White, Students with
Disabilities/College and Career (M4.9)
Improve systems for student progress monitoring and support through
the roles and responsibilities of the Student Support Facilitator. The
Student Support Facilitator will work collaboratively with the School
Principal and school staff to:
● Analyze student data and work to address areas of low
performance principally directed to support the needs of
specified student groups..
● Monitor student progress and develop interventions.
● Create systems and procedures for increased credit recovery
opportunities.
● Work closely with individual students and parents in creating
plans for academic success and increasing engagement
opportunities.
● Ensure the school and classrooms are properly resourced to
facilitate meaningful learning.
Provide teacher coaching, support, and professional development,
including any need to address support needed from teachers of out-of-
field misassignments (i.e., subject matter preparation or credentialing
needs).

actions.title: Progress Monitoring
actions.description:
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, Socio-Economically Disadvantaged,
Hispanic/ELA and Math (M4.7/M4.8)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White/Graduation (M4.2)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White, Students with
Disabilities/College and Career
Implement an internal and meaningful assessment and data collection
system to measure student growth and drive instructional practices.

actions.title: Social-emotional Well-being
actions.description:
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, Socio-Economically Disadvantaged,
Hispanic/ELA and Math (M4.7/M4.8)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White/Graduation (M4.2)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White, Students with
Disabilities/College and Career (M4.9)
● Provide appropriate student support for social-emotional,
mental, behavioral, nutritional, and physical wellness.
Full-time case managers from Project Hope Alliance and Stand Up For
Kids work one-on-one with students to develop plans designed to
address barriers to academic progress and high school graduation,
including academic support, mentoring, coordination with teachers for
progress monitoring, connections to external resources (such as
housing and healthcare), parent involvement, college and career
support, and 24/7 access to case managers. Agencies specialize in
supporting students experiencing homelessness or other challenges
experienced by socio-economically disadvantaged students.

actions.title: Expanded Day Opportunities
actions.description:
Specific actions to address areas of lowest performance:
Dashboard Focus (and associated metric)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, Socio-Economically Disadvantaged,
Hispanic/ELA and Math (M4.7/M4.8)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White/Graduation (M4.2)
Valley Vista HS/All Students, English Learners, Homeless, Socio-
economically Disadvantaged, Hispanic, White, Students with
Disabilities/College and Career (M4.9)
Implement expanded learning opportunities, including intervention
programs in a variety of content areas, additional credit recovery
opportunities, and summer school.

actions.title: Supplemental Resources
actions.description:
Enrich student experiences in the classroom through the use of
supplemental curriculum resources for academic core and elective
courses, including supplemental classroom instructional materials,
equipment, and upgraded technology. Provide other necessary
materials/supplies to support student wellness, support student
transportation needs, and to mitigate other barriers to student success.
